title: Is Living With a Random Roommate a Recipe for Disaster?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You fill out the housing form. You check the box: 'Assign me anyone.' Then you panic. What if they're messy? What if they're loud? What if they hate you? The horror stories are legendary. But most random roommate situations work out fine. Here is how to increase your odds of peaceful coexistence.

The Odds Are in Your Favor

For every roommate horror story, there are 100 boring stories where two people lived together, were polite, and went their separate ways. You just don't hear about those. The drama is loud. The success is quiet.

Most college students live with a random roommate at some point. Most survive. Many become friends. A few become enemies. Your odds of disaster are lower than your anxiety suggests.

The roommate matching algorithms use your preferences. Sleep schedule. Cleanliness. Study habits. They're not perfect, but they're not random either.

The First Conversation Is the Most Important

Before you move in, text or call. Introduce yourself. Ask basic questions. 'What time do you usually sleep?' 'Are you a morning person or night owl?' 'How do you feel about guests?'

Don't wait until move-in day to discover conflicts. Talk early. Set expectations before you're exhausted and surrounded by boxes.

This conversation is not a binding contract. People change. But it opens communication. It establishes that you're willing to talk about issues before they become crises.

Create a Roommate Agreement (Yes, Really)

Write it down. It doesn't have to be formal. A shared Google Doc is fine. Topics to cover:

• Sleeping hours (quiet hours)
• Cleaning schedule (who does what, when)
• Guests (overnight policy, notice required)
• Sharing items (food, supplies, electronics)
• Study time (when is the room off-limits for socializing)
• Temperature preferences (someone will always be cold, someone will always be hot)

This document is not a weapon. It's a reference. When a conflict arises, you can look back and say 'we agreed to quiet hours at 11 PM.'

When Conflicts Arise, Talk Early

Don't let resentment build. If your roommate does something annoying, say something. Not aggressively. Casually. 'Hey, would you mind using headphones after 11? I have trouble sleeping with noise.'

Most people are not malicious. They are oblivious. They don't know they're bothering you because you haven't told them. Assume ignorance, not ill intent.

If the problem continues, bring it up again. 'I know we talked about this before, but I'm still having trouble sleeping. Can we figure out a solution?'

When to Get the RA Involved

If you've talked multiple times and nothing changes, talk to your Resident Advisor (RA). They are trained to mediate conflicts. They can help facilitate a conversation. They can also help you switch rooms if necessary.

Talking to the RA is not tattling. It's using resources. Your RA is paid to help with exactly these situations. Use them.

If the behavior is extreme (theft, threats, harassment), go to the RA immediately. Don't wait. Your safety matters more than politeness.

You Don't Have to Be Best Friends

The best roommate is not your soulmate. The best roommate is someone who respects your space, cleans up after themselves, and communicates politely. Friendship is a bonus, not a requirement.

If you become close friends, great. If you don't, that's fine too. You can live peacefully with someone you rarely talk to. You're sharing a room, not a life.

Living with a random roommate is a skill. Like any skill, it improves with practice. You'll make mistakes. You'll learn. By sophomore year, you'll be a pro. And you'll have stories either way.
